Write better VBA. Debug faster. Build with confidence.

AIN1 Excel Pro is a free Excel engineering workspace for VBA review, formula analysis, workbook diagnostics, structured learning, and guided improvement — presented through a cleaner public landing experience before the full tool opens.

Free accessNo pricing wall. Anyone can open the workspace and start exploring the tool.

Analyzer canvas

WorkbookAnalyzer.bas — AIN1 Excel ProLive monitoring active
' Automated risk scan with guided fixes · AIN1 Analysis Engine
Sub AnalyzeWorkbook()
  Dim lastRow As Integer
  lastRow = Cells(Rows.Count, "A").End(xlUp).Row
  If lastRow > 0 Then
    Range("G2").Formula = "=IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2,Data!A:B,2,FALSE),"N/A")"
    riskScore = CalcRisk(lastRow)
  End If
End Sub

Models

Four reasoning layers behind the workspace.

Each layer supports a different kind of reasoning inside the tool so analysis stays structured instead of guessing.

Layer 01

Static Analysis Core

Parses VBA and formula structures to catch dead code, weak patterns, and logic shape issues before execution.

Structural review
Layer 02

Guided Review Engine

Prioritises issues, explanations, and safe next steps so findings stay readable instead of overwhelming.

Review clarity
Layer 03

Formula Dependency Graph

Maps workbook relationships so circular references, fragile chains, and optimization targets become easier to understand.

Workbook coverage
Layer 04

Proof-Based Validator

Supports a zero-false-pass mindset by challenging logic paths before anything is treated as truly clean.

Confidence layer
Workflow

One workflow from first scan to final review.

From pasted VBA to reviewed output, every stage is designed to stay inside a single continuous experience.

📊 Excel Criteria Expressions
🎯 What Are Criteria Expressions?
Criteria expressions are special text strings used in Excel functions like COUNTIF, SUMIF, AVERAGEIF to filter and analyze data. They combine operators (=, >, <, <>, etc.) with values to create conditional logic within formulas.
⌨️ Excel Keyboard Shortcuts
🎯 Master Excel with Keyboard Shortcuts
This comprehensive guide covers all essential Excel keyboard shortcuts organized by category. Compatible with Excel 2010, 2013, 2016, 2019, and Excel 365.
📑 Table Of Contents
SectionDescription
General / FileNew, Open, Save, Print, Close
Editing & FormulasEdit cells, Fill, Date/Time entry
NavigationJump, Go To, Find, Replace
SelectionExtend, Select all, Rows, Columns
FormattingBold, Italic, Number formats, Borders
Rows / ColumnsInsert, Delete, Hide, Unhide
Data & TablesSort, Filter, Table, Pivot
Formulas & FunctionsEvaluate, Name Manager, AutoSum
VBA / DeveloperAlt+F11, Macro, Immediate window
Version NotesNew shortcuts in Excel 365
1️⃣ GENERAL / FILE OPERATIONS
ShortcutAction
Ctrl + NCreate new workbook
Ctrl + OOpen workbook
Ctrl + SSave workbook
Ctrl + PPrint
Ctrl + WClose workbook
Ctrl + F4Close workbook (alternative)
Ctrl + TabSwitch between open workbooks
Alt + F4Close Excel
2️⃣ EDITING & FORMULAS
ShortcutAction
F2Edit active cell
Ctrl + EnterFill selected cells with current entry
Ctrl + DFill Down
Ctrl + RFill Right
Ctrl + `Show/hide formulas
Alt + EnterInsert line break in cell
Ctrl + ;Insert current date
Ctrl + Shift + :Insert current time
3️⃣ NAVIGATION
ShortcutAction
Ctrl + ↑/↓/←/→Jump to edge of data region
Ctrl + HomeGo to cell A1
Ctrl + EndGo to last used cell
Ctrl + Page Up/DownSwitch between worksheets
Ctrl + GGo To dialog
Ctrl + FFind
Ctrl + HFind and Replace
4️⃣ SELECTION
ShortcutAction
Shift + ↑/↓/←/→Extend selection by one cell
Ctrl + Shift + ↑/↓/←/→Extend selection to edge of data
Ctrl + ASelect all / current region
Ctrl + SpaceSelect entire column
Shift + SpaceSelect entire row
Ctrl + Shift + EndExtend selection to last used cell
5️⃣ FORMATTING
ShortcutAction
Ctrl + 1Format Cells dialog
Ctrl + BBold
Ctrl + IItalic
Ctrl + UUnderline
Ctrl + 5Strikethrough
Alt + H, O, IAutoFit column width
Ctrl + Shift + $Apply currency format
Ctrl + Shift + %Apply percentage format
6️⃣ ROWS / COLUMNS
ShortcutAction
Ctrl + +Insert cells/rows/columns
Ctrl + -Delete cells/rows/columns
Ctrl + Shift + +Insert cells dialog
Alt + H, D, RDelete row
Alt + H, D, CDelete column
Ctrl + 9Hide selected rows
Ctrl + 0Hide selected columns
7️⃣ DATA & TABLES
ShortcutAction
Ctrl + TCreate Table
Ctrl + Shift + LToggle AutoFilter
Alt + ↓Open filter dropdown (in header)
Alt + A, M, MRemove duplicates
Alt + A, S, SSort dialog
Alt + D, PPivotTable (legacy)
8️⃣ FORMULAS & FUNCTIONS
ShortcutAction
= then TabAuto-complete function name
Shift + F3Insert Function dialog
Ctrl + Shift + EnterArray formula (legacy)
F4Toggle absolute/relative references ($A$1, A$1, $A1, A1)
F9Calculate all sheets in all workbooks
Shift + F9Calculate active sheet
Ctrl + Alt + F9Force calculate all (even unchanged)
9️⃣ VBA / DEVELOPER
ShortcutAction
Alt + F11Open VBA Editor (VBE)
F5Run macro / Continue execution
F8Step Into (debug line by line)
Shift + F8Step Over (skip function)
Ctrl + Shift + F8Step Out (exit function)
Ctrl + BreakStop macro execution
F9Toggle breakpoint (in VBE)
Ctrl + GShow Immediate Window (in VBE)
🔟 EXCEL VERSION COMPATIBILITY

✅ All shortcuts above work in:

  • Excel 2010
  • Excel 2013
  • Excel 2016
  • Excel 2019
  • Excel 365 (Microsoft 365)

📝 Notes:

  • Ctrl + Shift + Enter for array formulas is legacy - Excel 365 uses dynamic arrays automatically
  • Ctrl + 0 (hide columns) may be disabled in some Excel 2010 versions
  • Alt key shortcuts (e.g., Alt + H, O, I) are Ribbon-based and work in Excel 2007+
  • Mac users: Replace Ctrl with ⌘ (Command) and Alt with ⌥ (Option)

📌 Absolute Cell References (F4)
Absolute Cell References: The 4 Reference Types Explained
Excel references change when you copy formulas — unless you “lock” the row and/or column using $. The F4 key cycles through the 4 reference types for the selected cell reference inside the formula bar.
Title What you’ll learn
Definitions Relative vs absolute vs mixed references
F4 cycle (4 types) How Excel toggles A1 → $A$1 → A$1 → $A1
Copy behavior What changes when copied right/down/diagonal
Example 1: Fixed price cell Quantity × $PriceCell
Example 2: Multiplication table Mixed references for row/column headers
Common mistakes How to quickly fix broken copies
Cheat sheet Fast summary of the 4 types

1) Definitions

  • Relative reference (A1) — changes row and column when copied.
  • Absolute reference ($A$1) — locks both column and row; never changes when copied.
  • Mixed reference (A$1 or $A1) — locks only row or only column.

2) The F4 toggle cycle (the 4 reference types)

Click the cell reference inside the formula bar (or highlight it in the formula), then press F4 to cycle: A1 → $A$1 → A$1 → $A1 → (back to A1). On some laptops, use Fn + F4.
Type Locks Copy right Copy down Copy diagonal
A1 None A1 → B1 A1 → A2 A1 → B2
$A$1 Column + Row $A$1 → $A$1 $A$1 → $A$1 $A$1 → $A$1
A$1 Row only A$1 → B$1 A$1 → A$1 A$1 → B$1
$A1 Column only $A1 → $A1 $A1 → $A2 $A1 → $A2

3) Worked example 1 — Fixed price cell

Scenario: Column A has Quantity, cell E1 has a fixed Unit Price. You want Quantity × Price for every row.
Cell Formula typed After copy down 1 row Why
B2 =A2*$E$1 =A3*$E$1 A2 becomes A3 (relative), $E$1 stays locked
B2 (wrong) =A2*E1 =A3*E2 E1 shifts to E2, breaking the fixed price reference

4) Worked example 2 — Multiplication table (mixed references)

Scenario: Row 1 contains headers (1–10), Column A contains headers (1–10). In B2, you want: Row header × Column header.
Put this in B2: =$A2*B$1 then fill across and down.
  • $A2 locks column A (row can change as you fill down).
  • B$1 locks row 1 (column can change as you fill right).

5) Common mistakes (and quick fixes)

  • Locked the wrong part: If your price moves when copying, lock it with $ (usually $E$1).
  • Everything is locked: If results don’t change when filling, you probably used $A$1 where A1 was needed.
  • Mixed reference swapped: If your multiplication table is wrong, check that column header uses $A2 and row header uses B$1.
Cheat sheet
A1 — moves row + column
$A$1 — locks both
A$1 — locks row only
$A1 — locks column only
Tip: Click the reference in the formula bar, then press F4 to cycle. (Some laptops: Fn + F4.)
Back to top
⚠️ Common & Complete Errors in Excel Formulas
📖 All-in-One Formula Error Reference
Every Excel formula error explained with causes, solutions, and examples. Covers core errors, dynamic array errors (365+), and common "not-an-error-code" pitfalls.
📑 Table Of Contents
SectionDescription
#DIV/0!Division by zero or empty cell
#N/AValue not found in lookup
#NAME?Unrecognized function or name
#NULL!Invalid range intersection
#NUM!Invalid numeric value / out of range
#REF!Broken or deleted reference
#VALUE!Wrong data type in operation
#SPILL!Dynamic array blocked (365+)
#CALC!Calculation engine error (365+)
#GETTING_DATAExternal data still loading
#FIELD! #CONNECT! #BLOCKED! #BUSY!Other environment-specific errors
Common Pitfalls (no error code)Syntax mistakes, circular refs, wrong types, locale issues, performance traps
1️⃣ #DIV/0! — Division by Zero
Symptom: Cell shows #DIV/0!
Tags: Math
Causes:
  • Dividing by a cell that contains 0 or is empty
  • A formula like =A1/B1 where B1 is blank (blank = 0)
  • AVERAGE/AVERAGEIF of an empty range (divides sum by 0 count)
  • A denominator formula that evaluates to zero at runtime
Solutions:
  • Guard: =IF(B1=0, "", A1/B1)
  • Wrap: =IFERROR(A1/B1, "N/A")
  • Use LET() to calculate denominator once and check it
  • Ensure data source cells are populated before referencing
❌ Bad:
=A1/B1 (B1 is blank)
✅ Good:
=IF(B1=0,"",A1/B1)
↑ Back to top
2️⃣ #N/A — Value Not Available
Symptom: Cell shows #N/A
Tags: Lookup
Causes:
  • VLOOKUP/HLOOKUP/MATCH cannot find the lookup value
  • VLOOKUP without 4th arg defaults to approximate match (TRUE) — requires sorted data
  • MATCH without 3rd arg defaults to match_type 1 — requires sorted ascending data
  • Leading/trailing spaces or hidden characters in lookup values
  • Data type mismatch: looking up number "123" (text) vs 123 (number)
  • INDEX(MATCH()) where MATCH returns #N/A
Solutions:
  • Always use exact match: =VLOOKUP(A1, range, 2, FALSE)
  • Wrap: =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(...), "Not found") or =IFNA(...)
  • Use XLOOKUP which defaults to exact match and has an [if_not_found] parameter
  • Clean data: =TRIM(CLEAN(A1)) before lookup
  • Force type: =VLOOKUP(VALUE(A1), ...) or =VLOOKUP(TEXT(A1,"0"), ...)
❌ Bad:
=VLOOKUP(A1,B:C,2)
(approx match, unsorted)
✅ Good:
=IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A1,B:C,2,FALSE),"")
↑ Back to top
3️⃣ #NAME? — Unrecognized Name
Symptom: Cell shows #NAME?
Tags: Syntax Text
Causes:
  • Misspelled function name: =SUMM(A1:A10) instead of =SUM()
  • Missing quotes around text: =IF(A1=Sales,...) instead of =IF(A1="Sales",...)
  • Using a function not available in your Excel version (e.g., XLOOKUP in Excel 2016)
  • Referencing an undefined named range
  • Localized function names in wrong locale (WENN vs IF, SVERWEIS vs VLOOKUP)
  • Missing add-in (e.g., Analysis ToolPak functions)
Solutions:
  • Check spelling — use autocomplete (type =SU and Tab)
  • Enclose text literals in double quotes: "Sales"
  • Define missing named ranges: Formulas → Name Manager
  • Check Excel version compatibility
❌ Bad:
=SUMM(A1:A10)
✅ Good:
=SUM(A1:A10)
↑ Back to top
4️⃣ #NULL! — Invalid Intersection
Symptom: Cell shows #NULL!
Tags: References
Causes:
  • Using a space between two ranges that don't intersect: =SUM(A1:A10 B1:B10)
  • Space is the intersection operator — if ranges don't overlap, result is #NULL!
  • Accidentally typing a space instead of a comma between arguments
Solutions:
  • Use comma for union: =SUM(A1:A10,B1:B10)
  • Use colon for continuous range: =SUM(A1:B10)
  • Remove unintended spaces between range references
❌ Bad:
=SUM(A1:A10 B1:B10)
✅ Good:
=SUM(A1:A10,B1:B10)
↑ Back to top
5️⃣ #NUM! — Invalid Number
Symptom: Cell shows #NUM!
Tags: Math
Causes:
  • SQRT(-1) — square root of negative number
  • LOG(0) or LN(-5) — logarithm of zero or negative
  • ACOS(2) / ASIN(2) — trig functions outside [-1,1]
  • Result too large (exceeds ±1.8×10³⁰⁸) or too small
  • IRR() or RATE() cannot converge to a solution
Solutions:
  • Validate inputs: =IF(A1>=0, SQRT(A1), "")
  • Use ABS: =SQRT(ABS(A1))
  • Clamp trig args: =ACOS(MIN(1,MAX(-1,A1)))
  • For IRR: provide a closer guess parameter
❌ Bad:
=SQRT(-1)
✅ Good:
=IF(A1>=0,SQRT(A1),"")
↑ Back to top
6️⃣ #REF! — Broken Reference
Symptom: Cell shows #REF!
Tags: References
Causes:
  • Deleted a row/column/sheet that a formula referenced
  • VLOOKUP col_index larger than the table range width
  • Copy-pasting formula with relative refs beyond sheet boundaries
  • Invalid range syntax: A1::A10 (double colon) or A0
  • INDIRECT pointing to a deleted sheet
Solutions:
  • Undo immediately: Ctrl+Z after accidental deletion
  • Use INDIRECT for dynamic sheet references
  • Use structured table references: Table1[Column]
  • Check VLOOKUP col_index doesn't exceed range width
  • Find & Replace #REF! to locate all broken refs
❌ Bad:
=SUM(#REF!)
(after deleting referenced range)
✅ Good:
=SUM(Table1[Sales])
(structured reference survives edits)
↑ Back to top
7️⃣ #VALUE! — Wrong Data Type
Symptom: Cell shows #VALUE!
Tags: Text Math
Causes:
  • Math operation on text: ="hello"+1
  • Numbers stored as text (green triangle indicator) in formulas expecting numbers
  • DATEVALUE/TIMEVALUE with invalid date/time string
  • Array formula size mismatch in older Excel (pre-365)
  • Passing a range to a function expecting a single value (pre-365)
Solutions:
  • Convert text to number: =VALUE(A1) or multiply by 1: =A1*1
  • Use NUMBERVALUE for locale-safe conversion
  • TRIM/CLEAN to remove hidden characters
  • Use --A1 (double unary minus) to coerce text-number
  • Data → Text to Columns → Finish (quick bulk convert)
❌ Bad:
="hello"+1
✅ Good:
=VALUE(A1)+1
↑ Back to top
Section B — Modern / Dynamic Array Errors (Excel 365+)
8️⃣ #SPILL! — Blocked Spill Range
Symptom: Cell shows #SPILL!. Blue dashed border around intended output range.
Tags: Dynamic Arrays
Causes:
  • FILTER, SORT, UNIQUE, SEQUENCE, etc. need empty cells below/right to "spill" results
  • Another value, formula, or even a space character is in the spill range
  • Merged cells blocking the output area
  • Spill range extends beyond worksheet boundaries
Solutions:
  • Click the error cell → blue border shows blocked area → clear those cells
  • Unmerge cells in the output area
  • Use @ operator if you only need one value: =@SORT(A1:A10)
  • Move the formula to a location with enough empty space
❌ Bad:
=SORT(A1:A10)
(cells below contain data)
✅ Good:
Clear cells below, or
=@SORT(A1:A10)
↑ Back to top
9️⃣ #CALC! — Calculation Error
Symptom: Cell shows #CALC!
Tags: Dynamic Arrays
Causes:
  • =FILTER(A:A, A:A<>"") when all values are empty → returns empty array
  • SORT/UNIQUE on an empty range
  • Complex nested dynamic array formulas producing ambiguous results
  • LAMBDA recursion exceeding limits
Solutions:
  • FILTER: use 3rd arg for empty: =FILTER(A:A, cond, "No results")
  • Wrap: =IFERROR(FILTER(...), "No data")
  • Simplify nested formulas step by step
❌ Bad:
=FILTER(A1:A10,B1:B10="X")
(no matches → #CALC!)
✅ Good:
=FILTER(A1:A10,B1:B10="X","None")
↑ Back to top
🔟 #GETTING_DATA — Waiting for Data
Symptom: Cell temporarily shows #GETTING_DATA
Tags: External Data
Causes:
  • External data connection (Power Query, OLAP, web query) still loading
  • STOCKHISTORY or other online functions waiting for server response
  • RTD (Real-Time Data) function waiting for COM server
Solutions:
  • Wait a few seconds — usually resolves automatically
  • Data → Refresh All (Ctrl+Alt+F5)
  • Check network connection
  • If persistent: check Data → Queries & Connections for errors
↑ Back to top
1️⃣1️⃣ #FIELD! #CONNECT! #BLOCKED! #BUSY!
Note: These errors appear in specific Excel 365 environments and may not be visible in all versions.
Details:
  • #FIELD! — A field reference in a linked data type (Stocks, Geography) is invalid. Fix: refresh the data type or re-select the field.
  • #CONNECT! — The data connection for a linked data type failed. Fix: check internet, re-link data type.
  • #BLOCKED! — A calculation is blocked by security policy or pending user action. Fix: enable content, check Trust Center settings.
  • #BUSY! — The calculation engine is overloaded (rare). Fix: wait, reduce complexity, or break formulas into steps.
↑ Back to top
Section C — Common Pitfalls (No Error Code, But Still Wrong)
1️⃣2️⃣ Common Formula Pitfalls & Silent Errors
🔸 Missing/Extra Parentheses
Excel may auto-add missing closing parens, giving wrong grouping. Always count: opening ( must equal closing ). Use formula bar highlighting to verify nesting.
🔸 Missing/Extra Commas or Semicolons
=IF(A1,,B1) — empty 2nd argument silently returns 0. Use =IF(A1,"",B1) for explicit empty string. In non-US locales, semicolons are the separator; mixing them causes errors.
🔸 Unclosed Quotes
=IF(A1="hello,B1,C1) — missing closing quote. Excel shows "There's a problem with this formula". Always pair every opening " with a closing ".
🔸 Wrong Absolute/Relative References ($)
Copying =A1/B1 down shifts both references. Use =A1/$B$1 to lock the denominator. Press F4 to cycle through reference modes: A1 → $A$1 → A$1 → $A1.
🔸 Circular References
A formula references its own cell directly or via a chain. Excel shows a warning dialog. Fix: Formulas → Error Checking → Circular References to locate the loop. Indirect circular refs through multi-cell chains are harder to find.
🔸 Implicit Intersection (@) — 365 vs Legacy
In Excel 365, =SUM(A:A) works as expected. In pre-365, passing a column to a single-cell function triggers implicit intersection (only the cell in the same row is used). The @ operator makes this explicit: =@A:A. Opening old workbooks in 365 may add @ automatically.
🔸 Numbers Stored as Text / Dates as Text
Cell shows a number left-aligned with green triangle → it's text. SUM/AVERAGE will skip it silently. Fix: select → Data → Text to Columns → Finish. Or =VALUE(A1), =DATEVALUE(A1).
🔸 Lookup Pitfalls
VLOOKUP approximate match (TRUE/omitted) requires sorted data in the first column — silently returns wrong results if unsorted. Always use exact match (FALSE) unless you specifically need approximate. XLOOKUP defaults to exact match and is safer.
🔸 Range / Union / Intersection Operator Mistakes
Colon : = range (A1:B10). Comma , = union (A1:A10,C1:C10). Space = intersection. Accidentally using space instead of comma gives #NULL! or wrong results.
🔸 Structured Reference Mistakes
=SUM(Table1[Sales]) works, but =SUM(Table1[sale]) (wrong column name) gives #REF!. Renaming a table column breaks external formulas referencing the old name. Use autocomplete inside brackets.
🔸 Locale Separator Issues
US/UK Excel uses comma , as argument separator. European locales (German, French, Dutch) use semicolon ;. Copying formulas between locales may break them. Excel auto-converts when you open/paste, but not always reliably in VBA or web imports.
🔸 Volatile Functions — Performance Traps
Functions like OFFSET(), INDIRECT(), TODAY(), NOW(), RAND(), RANDBETWEEN() recalculate every time any cell changes — not just their inputs. Hundreds of OFFSET/INDIRECT calls cause extreme slowness. Replace OFFSET with INDEX, INDIRECT with direct references or XLOOKUP where possible.
🔸 Wrong Operator Order / Unintended Concatenation
=1+2*3 gives 7 (not 9) — Excel follows math precedence. Use parentheses: =(1+2)*3. Also: =A1&B1 concatenates as text even if A1 and B1 are numbers — use =A1+B1 for addition.
↑ Back to top

👨‍💻 About the Author

Created by: Matthew Naliponguit Sugang

AIN1 Excel Pro is a FREE, user-friendly and a comprehensive tool for VBA and Formula development that guides user in learning and mastering a new set of skills in Excel using this tool. It has another set of features that will "Detect Excel formula Errors" and "Code execution Testing". It features advanced syntax highlighting, live error detection, intelligent autocomplete, comprehensive guide-notes, pre-made templates and many more.
